sentences.text: Eva Weiss.
E-V-A W-E-I-S-S.
At birth was Lefkovits, Eva Lefkovits, L-E-F-K-O-V-I-T-S.
And born in Hungary, Olaszliszka, 1928, December 10.
OK.
O-L-A-S-Z-L-I-S-Z-K-A.
[SIGHS] 66.
Well, Olaszliszka was a very small town, with a population about 3,800, 4,000 at most, and about 60 to 65 Jewish families.
So in my time, it was very hard, because there were only two other Jewish girls were same age as us.
So we went to Protestant school or we went to-- I graduated from eighth grade.
From fifth grade on, I went to a Catholic school.
And we always knew that we were different, but I wasn't really processing it.
It wasn't really hurt.
It's just I had to always be good in school, that I should be noticed or I should be belonging to the crowd.
And Jewish friends was very little, really-- just one girl in the whole town.
So every friend of mine were all Gentiles until about 1940, which I remember, when things started.
The war was going on.
We heard all horror stories, but we didn't believe it.
And then they took away my father's occupation.
They actually confiscated the butcher shop.
Well, it was-- it was a very close community and very close, family-oriented place, because we sort of all stick together.
I went to school.
After school I would play ball, or-- or summertime, swim.
And to-- then to pass time, I read, or I would just do some silly things, just playing outside with the girls when I could.
Or I just visited the family.
We were, like, four-- my father's four sister-- two sisters-- three sisters-- family lived in there.
And all the girls were older, and I always loved to hang around between them, watching them putting on lipstick, watching them, you know, all the things what they were doing.
So I really [INAUDIBLE] just always hung around in one family or another.
Some of my cousins were 18, and I was 15, so I really looked up to them-- you know, 20.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's about it.
It really, truly was a very quiet life.
It was a small town life.
There wasn't much entertainment.
When I went to the [INAUDIBLE] in 1944, that's my really recollection, the last winter-- 1943.
And I spent three months there.
So I went to shows, I went to movies, I went ice skating.
That was a little bit, you know, more adventurous, and I really loved it.
But it was a short time.
My two older brothers were gone, as I remember, already, you know, since '42.
And they both were on their own.
Both were in Pest-- Budapest.
And my sister mostly, all her growing up, all her adult age, she spent in Debrecen, which was a large city, the second largest city to Budapest.
Debrecen is D-E-B-R-E-C-E-N.
Oh, my sister was Edith, and my brother was Laszlo and Bela.
And my two younger brothers and I were most of the time home.
That's how I remember.
And my younger brother-- my older brother, two years older than me, was Sandor, and the youngest was Erno.
OK.
He was 13 at the time we left home.
Yes, yes.
In Europe, we were always close, you know, with the-- with the aunts and uncles, and-- and there was disagreement between the family, but the children always were close, you know.
[SIGHS] You know what?
My brothers, as I remember, from school, they had to go to-- they didn't really have a [INAUDIBLE], a Hebrew school, but sort of.
They made a group.
And they spent til-- til 6:00, 7:00 night there.
So I would have, after school, a few hours.
If I didn't do homework, I read or I just went to play-- try to play with other girls and visit.
And just most of the time play ball.
Wintertime, we went ice, you know, skating, because we had a flowing river in there, and we waited until it froze.
And we went sleighing.
And that was the entertainment, really.
It was nothing big.
Very simple life.
My parents were very hard working.
They were business people.
My father was the only Jewish-- well, the only butcher in a whole town.
And my recollection is this-- that he had to supply the whole town with meat, which was already, from 1942 or '41, rationed.
And they allowed like two, three cattles to kill for a week, and I don't know how many calves and how many sheep.
So he was a businessman.
He was constantly traveling from market to market, and he would buy more than one cattle or two and then sell it, you know, alive.
Then he would buy for the town what he had to kill for-- for meat for the-- you know, for the town.
So he was a lot gone.
A lot.
He would come home with the train, or with the wagon, late nights.
I remember my mother waiting up for him, set the table.
In Europe, you always set the table.
At least with the half table with the white tablecloth, plate, even if he came home 11:00 or 12:00.
And this is my really recollection.
And, of course, Shabbos he was home.
Saturday was the whole thing.
He always had to be home Saturday.
And Sunday, he would go afternoon, and he loved to play cards, and that was his entertainment.
And my mother just spent her time with us.
She was always around us.
[SIGHS] She was a very lovable person, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] not just for her children, but even when my brother went back to Hungary, the whole time.
They loved her.
She never hurt anyone.
She never raised her voice.
She was just great.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't think I ever got scolded by her.
My father, he was something else.
He was strict.
But my mother, she just lived for her children.
Well, I used to have her-- the-- my chores were always to dry the dishes.
Or after-- well, after breakfast I went to school every day, so it wasn't-- but when I was home, I always had to help her in the kitchen, just mainly because we washed dishes, you know, by hand.
And to dry the dishes, put it away, and clean.
And I should, you know, Thursdays if got-- anything came together, rain or shine, you must shine the real silver.
You must get ready for Shabbos, and that was the biggest thing.
Or, you know, in a kitchen, not too much, not so much cooking.
Maybe cleaning vegetables or something id I was around-- if I was home.
And the last-- the last winter I was at home, 1943, I felt like, you know, a teenager-- big girl.
And then when I came home January, then-- because my mother was very sick.
She had a major operation, which she survived to go into Auschwitz.
And then we just huddled around her, just taking care of her.
She was in bad, and my sister came home.
And, you know, she did all the cooking, taking care of the family, whatever it had to be.
And then I had to help quite a bit.
Then I helped.
Shabbat was just closeness.
You know, the kids would come home.
Like, my two brothers would always come home for the holidays, and there was a big doing, and my mother would prepare ahead.
And it was very hard work.
When you think back now, she made challah every single Friday.
She made bread every, I think, Thursday or Friday-- three big round loaves, because [INAUDIBLE] we were-- you know, when we got together, we were eight of us.
And she was an excellent cook.
She was excellent cook.
She could make a meal out of nothing.
When I think about it now, and it really was nothing, and I'm thinking back, how did they do it?
What was the feeling like in the house during the holidays?
Was it joyous?
Joyous.
Joyous.
My father would sit down, mainly my two-- well, even the older brothers when they came home, they all-- my brothers didn't keep the Jewishness as they-- as soon as they went out of the house.
You know, they all got a little bit more assimilated.
They didn't want to be religious.
But when they came home, they always had the respect to go to the synagogue with my father, and he was very proud.
They were grown tall, you know.
And my-- my brother, Bela, he already in there showed sign that he had really-- my father had to [LAUGHS] practically drag him to a synagogue.
But they were all around him, and it was a joyous occasion always.
And they prayed together, or after, you know, a meal, the boys would stay at the table.
I would help my mother, and they always said the prayers.
And they knew it.
It was actually winter.
I-- in 1943, I spent about three months with my grandfather's and my uncle's house.
That was in Debrecen, which was, you know, a large town.
It was a large city with the theaters-- with movie theaters-- everything.
Ballet, everything.
Opera.
It was really a beautiful, cosmopolitan city, and I hear it still is.
I didn't see it, but that's what they tell me.
It cha-- I think it was in 1940-- '40 or '41-- as soon as we heard the rumors that, already, Hitler is in Czechoslovakia, which started in 1938.
Nobody wanted to believe it.
And they drafted all the boys and all the Jewish boys.
And they took them out to Ukrainian border, because they draft them as Hungarian soldiers.
And later on, they strip them from their uniform, and they were just slave labor camp.
They were mainly working for-- you know, dig ditches or trenches and-- and railroad tracks.
And they were killing them right and left.
And a horror story came, but we were home.
Nobody believed it.
And things changed when a Jew could not go to public school.
It was a quota to take between every hundred kid, three Jewish kids-- three persons.
Three Jewish kids could go into higher education.
I think it started about '41, I'm pretty sure.
And that's when things started.

Then all of a sudden, one day my father gets a letter that he does not own his home, which he inherited from his father-- my grandfather-- the butcher shop, and they stripped him from legally owning a business.
So he actually had to pay, to the town, rent on his own property.
But because there was no other butcher-- just one and only in the small town, who slaughtered only pork, which my father never had, they had to have somebody running the show.
So they gave him back.
He was promoted, then, because he was in the First World War, and-- and he was a Russian prisoner, and God knows the stories they used to say.
And because of all of this, he finally got back his business.
And he got back the permit that he must be a butcher, and he must supply the town with their meat.
And, of course, they started to ration it even for the Gentiles and, of course, no kosher meat for a Jew.
That was the first thing they took away.
And my recollection out of all this, this is when I started to live in fear and-- and be scared, because my father would go.
He wouldn't just lie down and do nothing, so he would conduct like a [INAUDIBLE] He would bring-- he couldn't do the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the guy that-- what they call that?
The [NON-ENGLISH], I think, who slaughters the cow.
My father never did it.
And how do you spell [HEBREW].
[HEBREW] is a Jewish word, and I don't know what the-- even in-- well, in Hungarian, they call it [HUNGARIAN].
The one who studies-- just like a mohel-- who studies just to slaughter.
What they call it?
[HEBREW].
Oh, a [HEBREW], I think.
And slaughters the cows or cattles and all that.
And my father would take over after and, you know, cut it up and portion it, or they skin it and then sell the leather-- the skin to the leather guys, and the inside guts to another company.
And my father would go and from every-- well, at least once a month, he risked our family, he risked his life, and would bring [NON-ENGLISH] from a different town.
Then when he gets off the train, the town people cannot recognize him.
He's just another ordinary Jew coming to the little town.
And he would pick him up, bring him to the house, go to the barn and do just small things like a lamb or maybe two calves.
And then he would go, nighttime, in the butcher shop and sort it out.
And I don't know if you know that a half a meat is-- half side of the cattle is kosher and a half isn't.
So the half would go out to the Gentiles during the Sunday or whatever.
Three times a week he had to serve them.
And the rest he just portioned it out and sent them out by us, us kids-- my little brother, me-- because we were the ones that were home.
And little packages, and one went for the rabbi family, one went for the-- first was the family taken care of, the uncles and aunts.
And actually he spread it out that everybody got one or 2 kilos, which is like 2, 3 pounds of meat for every Jew in town.
My mother, from that point on, was just beyond herself, nervous and shaking.

I would stay outside at the gate and just, you know, play around with the ball and casually-- I was trained to just, you know, if I was suspicious, noticed somebody wants to come in our gate, I'd give them some kind of a signal.
While my father, in the back, in the barn doing this with the-- with the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's when I started to learn what fear is, because we really lived in fear.
But my father didn't want to give in.
He didn't want to give in.
He just felt that-- I mean, how much chicken and how much goose can you eat, and he had to do it.
And he risked-- this went on for about three years.
And he really risked our life and-- you know, as kids.
And that-- that's what constantly stays in my mind, how-- how really, he just didn't care.
I guess even if they would have arrested him at that point, I don't know.
But he had to do it.

He felt that because it was just so cruel for him to work seven days-- six, not seven days-- actually five days in a butcher shop, serve them their meat, and he deprived the children, of us and all the Jews, from a piece of calf or a piece of a lamb meat.
And all we had, legally, was chicken and goose.
Turkey was very rare there.
So actually we lived on chicken and goose.
This was like three, four years going on before then.
And he just couldn't take it.
And he says he doesn't care.
He risked it.
First the rabbi was against it, because it wasn't kosher enough, because he did it-- he did it in a barn.
And then he had to slice it up, you know, clean the counter part up where the [INAUDIBLE] meat was.
But in the end, when it sort of started to be going on for years, he already resorted, and he accepted the meat with my father, because they all paid for him for that, you know?
But just-- just a [INAUDIBLE].
At one point, my sister was very sick, and a doctor ordered that she must have red meat, must have beef.
My father would send the meat over, extra pounds, to the closest neighbor-- the Gentile people-- to cook it up, because heaven help we would cook, you know, [NON-ENGLISH].
That didn't go that far.
And just to feed my sister, Edith, because she was so anemic.
I remember that too.
So this is-- this is when I had-- and then he would always preach-- I wanted, when I was approaching finishing eighth grade, I wanted to go higher education.
I begged and cried, and my father's comment was, not a Jewish girl doesn't need education.
No, [INAUDIBLE].
Jewish-- you know-- and for my sister, they give-- but my sister and I have seven-- seven years between us, and seven days, to be exact.
And when my time came, the [INAUDIBLE] was already there.
So they did not concentrate that I should have the schooling, and I was the one who really begged them.
So finally, he resigned that, after the eighth grade, I will take courses summertime.
And if I-- if I passed, then I could go into a what they called [NON-ENGLISH], which was a third grade of, like-- how they call it?
It was like-- was it like high school?
Because after eighth grade, I think it would be high school, OK?
And so he went and privately paid a teacher who taught me all summer.
And sure enough, I had to learn geometry, arithmetic, German, and some other subject.
And I studied all summer.
Oh, I wanted to be the best.
And when we went to-- when my father took me in, which was a town where they had a gymnasium, which is like university college.
They had the high school.
And of course you had to pay.
That was not supplied by government or anything.
And that's where the quota was that only three Jews can attend the school publicly between 100.
And they didn't want to commute us too much.
They really cut.
And when I was cut down, and they said-- after, you know, the test and everything-- and sure enough, they wanted to just cut you down from mainly German.
I'll never forget.
And they said, sorry, I was in the first grade.
I didn't have all A's, and I wasn't good enough, and commuters they not take.
Well, I went home with my father just crying on the train, and I was just heart broken.
So that's why, when they didn't know what to do with me, so for that winter they let me go to my grandfather, because I was heart broken.
That was my biggest thing in my life.
I wanted higher education.
They wouldn't-- they wouldn't let me feel it in-- as a smaller girl.
I had to sit through, like, you know, when they had their Catholic-- what they call it-- Catechism, OK, and they had their religious program or prayers, I would-- we Jewish girls, we would just sit.
And there was not even a Jewish boy in our whole class, just the two girls.
We would just sit it through.
So, you know, we started to learn all the Latin with them.
I knew a lot of Latin at that time.
And then, in the choir, they needed more girls.
And I remember that-- there was a last grade, like in the eighth grade-- my mother let me be in a choir.
And I went into the Catholic church, because we were in already before, because they were not that-- my father was not that strict that I-- I did what the crowd did.
And I was so proud that I could join them in a choir and sing in a church that I cannot tell you.
That I belonged, like, you know, I was in a group.
You felt it, but you didn't-- you didn't think about it that much, because when there was homework, I was doing it with a Gentile girl.
I was better in arithmetic.
I helped her.
I went to their house.
They were nice.
They always would give us, like, cookie and all, and they knew that I was allowed to eat that or a glass of milk or something like that.
And they would come to our house, too.
But-- and doing homework, because that-- certain things when I was better, and I helped her.
And her name was Eva, too.
I'll never forget.
So they were nice.
But when Christmas came, I used to be so envious, because all the gifts they would get.
And, you know, in Europe we didn't do such a big deal out of Hanukkah like in here.
All we had is dreidel and a few pennies, and then we played the dreidel all, you know, seven days and light the candles.
And-- and they would get all those gifts and dolls, and I used to be so envious that I didn't have that.
So you always, actually, felt deep down that you're different.
It wasn't easy.
I'm sure it wasn't easy.
Changed.
Yes.
It did change for us.
Well, the [? gendarmes ?] were very [? strong-- ?] already they were starting.
We didn't experience it, like, personally, our home.
But every so often, the rabbis had-- the [INAUDIBLE] rabbi, we call it short [INAUDIBLE], was a very famous rabbi.
Went all over from the town, especially from the state, sampling.
They would come to him on holidays to spend with him.
And, you know, question the [INAUDIBLE] and everything.
And they would break their windows, you know.
One Saturday night, they went wild, and they would break the windows.
And my father would go and talk to the guys, you know, he would know at the city hall.
It didn't help.
It didn't help.
They already noticed that the Jew cannot do as much as they do-- the Gentiles.
And then what happened, that government, matter of fact, subsidized schooling for all those farm children.
And that's when it was hurting, that we couldn't even go for our money to get into same school, and here they're subsidized.
And they could go, like the villagers, or the farmer's son, was going out of town to go to high school or to college.
So the fear was-- we were actually feared that they shouldn't do harm to us.
And my father always used to preach, don't dress like you would be flashy.
Don't do anything that-- that-- that they notice that a Jew has a little more, or the Jew knows better, or something.
So we always had to be-- inside, we felt proud, and we knew we were better, special, because, you know, it's-- it's a different type of Gentile.
It's mostly farmers, really.
Because the merchant in town, all of them, I think back, were Jewish.
So we had to sell them everything.
But the farmers, all of them Gentiles.
And when they were coming up and things changing, and they benefited by life, and we were going down, it was very hard.
And they were keeping the Jews down in every sense, every sense.
So we had a certain amount of fear always that they shouldn't harm us or hurt us or anything.
Well, when it began to get worse is that we heard more and more stories.
You know, the newspaper would come to town.
My father was glued to it.
But as a child, I didn't pay too much attention, because the life was the same routine still.
And I knew they used to discuss it Saturdays, and-- and they used to talk about it.
And then, of course, 1940, he had a brother in Czechoslovakia, which one part was actually, beginning was, I think, Hungary when he moved there.
And he was very wealthy, and somehow word came that a [INAUDIBLE] started.
So he wanted to save his two children.
And my brother was about 18, what was 17?
He would meet-- the oldest brother would meet.
My father, you know, told him, you go to the border, which was [INAUDIBLE].
It was a border of Czechoslovakia, and they would try to escape to Hungary, which I heard quite a few people, they thought, you know, the [INAUDIBLE] will not come to Hungary.
They'll be safe.
And what happened is these two cousins of ours, they did come and they made it to Hungary.
They were hiding in Budapest, because they had no paper, you know, legally.
And those days, already, when you get on a train, you had to show your paper.
From one town to another, if I took the train, which I did, to [INAUDIBLE], I had to have, like, a little passport.
And of course they knew you were Jewish.
You had-- when you-- when they looked for the ticket, and you purchased the ticket, you had to show that.
When the conductor came on the train, you had to show it.
So they know you were Jewish.
So you were always, always kind of scary and shy away, and just behave very, very quietly, that they shouldn't even notice you.
So these two cousin of ours escaped to Hungary, were for a while in Budapest, and then they emigrated to-- they wanted to go to Israel.
At that time was Israel Palestine.
And they did go, and evidently, the history says-- I mean, they really found out the date, that they took that ship-- you know, the illegal ship, which the British confiscated.
Well, they died on the ship or they died in a camp there, we don't know.
But they never made it.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] that was it.
